question: [File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: responseCacheUpdateHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the deletion of VARY_HEADER in responseCacheUpdateHandler() (delete headers[VARY_HEADER]) before storing in Redis, causing the cached response to lack the Vary: accept-encoding header, and when served back by responseCacheCheckHandler() it re-adds Vary: accept-encoding, creating a header inconsistency that confuses downstream caches into serving the wrong encoding variant of staking reward data?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: responseCacheUpdateHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that responseCacheUpdateHandler() also caches HTTP 304 responses (isUnmodified check) by sending a conditional GET with a matching ETag to /api/v1/accounts/<id>/rewards, causing the cache to be updated with a 200 OK status code (statusCode = isUnmodified ? httpStatusCodes.OK.code : res.statusCode) but with headers from the 304 response that may be missing content-type, leading to malformed cached responses for staking reward data?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: cacheKeyGenerator()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the CACHE_KEY_VERSION_SUFFIX='-v1' appended to the MD5 hash in cacheKeyGenerator() by predicting cache key patterns for known account IDs and using Redis timing side-channels (if Redis is exposed) to enumerate which accounts have active staking reward cache entries, revealing which accounts are actively querying their rewards?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: responseCacheCheckHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user send a HEAD request to /api/v1/accounts/<id>/rewards to trigger the negotiate() path for HEAD requests (isHead branch) without consuming the response body, and by observing the Content-Length header returned, infer the size of the staking rewards response and deduce the approximate number of reward entries for the account?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: responseCacheCheckHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that responseCacheCheckHandler() logs req.ip, req.method, req.originalUrl including the account ID in plaintext (logger.info), and if log aggregation is accessible, enumerate all account IDs that have been queried for staking rewards, then target those accounts for cache timing attacks?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: responseCacheUpdateHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that responseCacheUpdateHandler() uses res.getHeaders() for non-304 responses, which may include server-side headers revealing internal infrastructure details, and these headers are stored in Redis and later re-served via responseCacheCheckHandler(), potentially leaking internal routing or version information that aids in targeting the staking rewards infrastructure?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: responseCacheCheckHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user send requests to /api/v1/accounts/<id>/rewards with different timestamp filter combinations (e.g., timestamp=gte:0, timestamp=lte:9999999999) to populate the cache with multiple snapshots of the staking reward history, and by comparing the cached responses over time, reconstruct the exact timing and amounts of all reward payouts for a victim account?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: cacheKeyGenerator()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that cacheKeyGenerator() uses req.originalUrl which includes query parameters, by sending /api/v1/accounts/1001/rewards?order=asc and /api/v1/accounts/1001/rewards?order=desc to create two separate cache entries, and by observing which entry is populated first (via ETag comparison), determine the order in which staking rewards were processed?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: responseCacheCheckHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the ETag-based conditional caching by first requesting /api/v1/accounts/<id>/rewards to obtain an ETag, then repeatedly sending if-none-match requests with that ETag to keep receiving 304 responses, preventing the cache from being refreshed with updated pending_reward data and causing the account owner to see stale reward information?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: shouldCompress()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the shouldCompress() function's threshold check (body.length >= config.cache.response.compressThreshold) by observing whether a staking rewards response is compressed or not (via Content-Encoding header), to infer whether the response body exceeds the compression threshold and thus deduce the approximate number of reward entries for a victim account?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: responseCacheUpdateHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the race condition between the cache check (responseCacheCheckHandler) and cache update (responseCacheUpdateHandler) by sending two simultaneous requests to /api/v1/accounts/<id>/rewards at a staking period boundary, causing both requests to miss the cache, both hit the database at different moments, and the later database result (with higher pending_reward) to be overwritten by the earlier result (with lower pending_reward) in Redis?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: responseCacheCheckHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the cache key does not include any session or user identity by requesting /api/v1/accounts/<victim_id>/rewards after the victim has already warmed the cache, receiving the victim's complete staking reward history (account_id, amount, timestamp for each reward) without any authentication?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: responseCacheCheckHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the public cache-control header to cause a CDN or reverse proxy to cache the /api/v1/accounts/<id>/rewards response, and then by requesting the same URL from a different geographic location, receive the cached staking reward data that was originally fetched by the account owner, leaking reward amounts and timestamps?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: cacheKeyGenerator()] Can an unprivileged external user send requests using different account ID formats (numeric 1001, shard.realm.num 0.0.1001, EVM address 0x00000000000000000000000000000000000003e9, alias KGNABD5L3ZGSRVUCSPDR7TONZSRY3D5OMEBKQMVTD2AC6JL72HMQ) to /api/v1/accounts/<id>/rewards, generating four separate cache entries for the same account, and by comparing the TTLs of each entry, determine when the account's reward data was last queried?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: responseCacheUpdateHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that responseCacheUpdateHandler() stores the entire response body (including all staking reward transfers with amounts and timestamps) in Redis without encryption, and if the Redis instance is accessible via a network misconfiguration, directly read all cached staking reward data for any account?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: responseCacheCheckHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the TTL returned from Redis (redisTtl) being set as the max-age in the cache-control response header, by observing the max-age value to determine how long ago the staking rewards response was cached, and thus infer the approximate time of the last reward query for a victim account?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: responseCacheCheckHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that responseCacheCheckHandler() serves the cached response body directly from Redis without re-validating the account's existence or reward eligibility, by requesting /api/v1/accounts/<deleted_account_id>/rewards after the account is deleted but before the cache expires, receiving staking reward data for a deleted account?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: negotiate()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request with a crafted Accept-Encoding header containing gzip with various quality values (e.g., gzip;q=0.001) to manipulate the Negotiator library's encoding selection in negotiate(), causing the server to serve the uncompressed body when the cache stores a compressed version, and by measuring the decompression overhead, infer the compressed-to-uncompressed ratio and thus the entropy of the staking rewards data?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: responseCacheUpdateHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the condition in responseCacheUpdateHandler() that caches responses when responseBody && responseCacheKey && (isUnmodified || httpStatusCodes.isSuccess(res.statusCode)), by triggering a 200 response with an empty rewards array for /api/v1/accounts/<id>/rewards just before a staking reward is recorded, causing the empty response to be cached and served to the account owner who expects to see their reward?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: cacheKeyGenerator()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the MD5 hash in cacheKeyGenerator() being deterministic and based solely on the URL, by pre-computing the cache keys for all known account IDs' /rewards endpoints and using a timing side-channel against Redis to determine which accounts have active cache entries, revealing which accounts are actively monitoring their staking rewards?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: responseCacheCheckHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that responseCacheCheckHandler() does not validate the integrity of the cached response (no HMAC or signature on the Redis value), and if an attacker can write to Redis (via SSRF or misconfiguration), inject a crafted CachedApiResponse with a falsified pending_reward value that will be served to the account owner?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: responseCacheCheckHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the Object.assign(new CachedApiResponse(), redisValue) deserialization in responseCacheCheckHandler() by injecting a malicious Redis value with prototype pollution payloads, potentially corrupting the CachedApiResponse object and causing incorrect staking reward data to be served?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: responseCacheUpdateHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that responseCacheUpdateHandler() merges config.response.headers.default with res.getHeaders() for 304 responses, and if the default headers include a long-lived cache-control directive, cause the staking rewards response to be cached for an extended period, hiding reward updates from the account owner?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: responseCacheCheckHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request to /api/v1/accounts/<id>/rewards with a timestamp filter that matches a specific staking period (e.g., timestamp=gte:1234567890.000000000&timestamp=lte:1234567890.999999999) to cache a response showing rewards for that period, and then use the cached ETag to determine whether any rewards were paid in that period without making additional database queries?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: responseCacheCheckHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the cache does not differentiate between requests from different IP addresses or users, by sending a request to /api/v1/accounts/<victim_id>/rewards from any IP address and receiving the victim's cached staking reward data, since the cache key is based only on the URL?
